Re
Fern Por-rrrcAL Pn,qcrrcES CoN,rMrssroN
.128 J Street . Suite 670 o Sacramenro, CA 95gl"l_2329
\916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
October 28,Z0L0
Pamela Bensoussan
Chula Vista City Council
276 Fourth Avenue
Chtrla Vista, California 95814
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-156
FACTS
You are a city council member of the city of Chula Vista and wish to vote on a proposed
ordinance setting forth procedures and criteria for the designation and delisting of properties as
Dear Ms. Bensoussan:
This letter is.in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (thel'Act").1 Please note thai our advice is based solely
on the provisions of the Act. we therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other
conflict-of-interest laws such as common law confliciof interest. Also, the Commission does not
act as a finder of facr in providing advice. (In re oglesby (1975) I Fppc ops.72)
QUESTTON
As a member of the Chula Vista city council, may you vote on the adoption of the
Historic Preservation ordinance that would.set forth a process and criteria foriesignation or
delisting of real property as historic properties if you own two houses that are designated as
historic properties?
CONCLUSION
If it is not reasonably foreseeable that the city council decision will have a material
financial effect on any of your economic interests, yl, rnoy vote on the matter without violating
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.
' 'the Politit'l Refbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections til000 through 9l0l.t. All statukrry
ref'erencesaretotheGovernmentCode,unlessothenviseindicated. TheregulationsoftheFairpolitical practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of ritle 2 of the Calitbrnia code of Regulations. All
re-eulabry ref-erences are to Title 2, Division 6 oi thi Calitbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. 4-10-156
Page No. 2
historic properties (the "ordinance"). You own two houses that are designated historic
properties, a rental house and the house in which yo,r."ria".
once a property is designated as a historic property, the owner may, pursuant to the Mills
Act' enter into an agreement trith the city requiridil;;"er to maintain-thi historic aspects of
the property' such as replacing a roof to comply *i-th .urr"nt fire ratings and replacing a 100-
year old sewer' as you have done. In exchange for the "*r* maintaining the property, the
property owner receives tax benefits from the city. rrre vtitts Act is irt"ia.J io provide a benefit
to the entire community by providing property tax relief to offset trre rrigrr clsts of maintaining
the property' There are currently 62-resid!r,i"r p-p"ny J*n.., whose properties are designated
as historic property. There or" ig,72o residential p."d; owners in the city.
The ordinance will set forth procedures and criteria for designating and delisting
property as a historic property' Youitate that the ordinance is intended to simply clarify current
requirements but also state that the ordinance may make it more difficult for a property to
qualify for designation as a historic property. You state that the ordinance *outa have no effect
ff$l?ff:ffi:ffillr1::'r*ted ai historic properries. vou ask whetheryou .uy vote on
ANALYSIs
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will ,perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused uv iir"ir own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (section g1001(b).) section g7100
prohibits any public official from makins, participating in iaking, or ott ".*1r" using his or her
,xf:::1.r"'ition 
to influence a sovemmentit decision i'n which th-e official hr;; financial
The commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 1g700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interesr exists *hr;l;iii ;prih" official makes a govemmentat
fl::::?i',X?:[|:J 
reasonabrv roreseeabre materiar ri#;i;i errect on one ;;;;.. or his or her
step 1. Are you a "pubric officiar, within the meaning of section g7r00?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or consurtant
of a state or local goy:Tm-ent agency."-As a city "orr.ii."mber, which i, a'local govemment
agency' you are a public official. Therefore, you-may not make, participate in making, or
otherwise use your position to make, participate tr;rk;;;r 
influence any clecisions that will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial "rrr"ior-ury of your economic interests.


File No. A-10-156
Page No. 3
Step 2. Will you be making, participating in making or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting within the
aLlthority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) In voting on the adoption of the Ordinance, you will be making a
governmental decision.
Step 3. What are your economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from
certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in Section 87103
and Regulations 18703-18703.5, inclusive:
o { public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section g7103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a).)
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(d); Regularion 18703. t(b).)
a
o
a
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct
or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Secrion 87103(b); Regulation Ig703.2.)
An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3.)
o A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
total $420 or more within l2 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); Regulation
18703.4.)
o I public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets,
or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. Thisls commonly referred
to as the "personal financial effects" mle. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)
Real Property:
As the owner of two properties in which you presllmably have an investment worth $2,000 or
more, you have an economic interest in these properties.


File No. A-10-156
Page No.4
Source of Income:
Because you own rental property for which you presumably will have received rent
payments of $500 or more within 12 months of voting on the Ordinance, your tenant is a source
of income to yorr.
Personal Finances:
A public official is considered to always have an economic interest in his or her personal
finances' However, a financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly
by a public official is not considered as a separate financial eflecion the official,s personal
finances and would not be analyzed separately under the "personal financial effects,,mle.
(Regulation 18705.5(a).) Accordingly, the personal financial effects rule does not appear to
apply to your circumstances and we will noi discuss it further.
Step 4' Will your economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in decisions you will
make, participate in making or influence as a pubtic official?
Real Prooern
Regulation 18704.2(a) sets forth circumstances under which real property is directly
involved in a governmental decision. subsection (l)(z)provides that:
"(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or leasi, or inclusiJn in or
exclusion frol yy city, county, district or other local govemmentar
subdivision, of the real property in which the official his an intercst or a
similar decision affecting the real property. For purposes of this
subdivision, the terms "zoning" and .,rezoning,'ihail refer to the act of
establishing or changing the zoning or land use designation on the real
property in which the official has an interest.,, (Emphasis added.)
Under the predecessor to Regulation 18704.2(a)(2),wehave advised that property owned by a
public official identified on a city's heritage resources inventory is directly involved in a
governmental decision to amend the heritage preservation ordinance to aliow property owners to
request removal of their property from the inventory. (GambordAdvice Letter, No. 4-97-042;
repealed Regulation 18702.1(aX3XA).) In our advice letter, we treated a decision involving the
inclusion or exclusion of the property on the list as essentially a decision involving the inclusion
or exclusion of property in a "district." The prior regulation provided that real property is
directly involved if the decision involves the zoning]o, ,"roning, annexation or deannexation,
sale, purchase' or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion from ony "ity, county, districtor other local
governmental agency.
Current Regulation ls70a-2@)(2) adds to this list any "similar decision affecting the real
property"' We interpret this regulation to apply to a decision to adopt the Ordinance on the basis


File No. A-10-156
Page No. 5
that a decision to list or delist property is similar to decisions involving inclusion or exclusion of
real property from o *y, .o,,rty, aittii"t or other local governmental Jubdivision. Therefore,
your real property is directly involved in the city councii's decision to oaoft trr" o.din*"r.
Under Regulation l87o4.l(a), a.persol, including a source of income, is directly involved
in a govemmental decision before on ofii"iol'r'og.n"yii'rhat person (i) initiates the proceeding
in which the decision will be made by filinga" olpri.officlaim, appeal, or similar reqlrest, or
(ii).is a lamed party in, or is the subject of,-the pio"r"airrg concerning the decision. your tenant
will not be initiating the proceeding, nor will he or she bJa named party in or the subject of the
proceeding conceming the decision. Accordingly, the tenant is indirectly involved in the
decision.
Source of Income:
Step 5. What is the appticable materiality standard?
Real Properg:
Regulation 18705'2(a) provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision for
directly involved real propertylt p."s,r*ed to be materiar. The-presfmpri*;"y be rebutted by
proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the gorc--"ntal decision will have any financial
effect on the real propertY-'^ (The one-penny rule.) -You have stated that the decision to adopt the
ordinance will have no--effect on youi."ul property because your property is already designated
as a historic property' However, ihe provisions ofirre ordinance addressing the delisting of
property would affect your real property if you were to decide to aetistitre p-p"ny. under these
circumstances, we cannot say that t-here will be no financial effect on your ieaf property and,
therefore, the materiality standard is met.
Source of Income:
The materiality test, for an individual who is a source of income that is indirectly
involved' is whether the governmental decision will arreciirre individual,s irrcome, investments,
or other assets or liabilities (other than real property) or, io. real property, including leased
propertv, bv $1,000 o. T,o.": (Regulations r-sz0s.riuX3) and tgios.zraj.i il;", not appear
that the ordinance would affect your tenant's incomq'investments, assets or liabilities nor would
;:l'fi:l''" 
tenant's interest in real property. Accordingry, the uppii"aut" maGality standard is


File No. A-10-156
Page No. 6
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the
governmental decision on your economic interests will meet that applicable materiality
standard?
An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made
depends on the specific facts surrounding the decisio n. (in re Thorner, supra.)
You have stated that "the Historic Preservation Ordinance does not change the status quo
vis-i-vis historic properties per se; it merely addresses how historical properties are identified
and creates a process to designate, de-list and protect them." In additi,on, you indicate that the
Ordinance "simply clarifies the criteria that is currently required for designation and establishes
findings of fact for designation, which, in essence *oy *ot" it more difficult for a property to
qualify for designation than is currently required." (Emphasis added.) Based on theie facts, it is
not substantially likely, and, therefore, not reasonably foreseeable that the city council decision
will have a material financial effect on your interestsin real property.
- 
Accordingly, you may vote on adoption of the Ordinance without violating the conflict-
of-interest provisions of the Act.
lf you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General
el
vJjgl
By: Valentina Joyce,
Counsel, Legal Division
